explicit_text: I was at our headquarters office when I first heard that a plane had crashed into one of the towers of the World Trade Center in New York. I quickly went to our communications center where CNN was tuned in, and I saw the second plane crash into the second tower live on TV. Watching both towers fall was surreal and shocking, especially knowing that some of my coworkers had family working there. The World Trade Center attacks were the catalyst for many decisions we made that day.

Watching both towers fall was surreal and shocking, especially knowing that some of my coworkers had family working there. Those attacks were the catalyst for many decisions we made that day.

explicit_text: New York City was at the center of the tragic events that unfolded on September 11, 2001. I watched live on CNN as planes crashed into the World Trade Center towers there, and the reactions of people in New York City were heartbreaking. Many of my coworkers had relatives working in the city, which made the situation feel very personal. The city’s trauma was something we all felt even from six hours away.

explicit_text: CNN was the channel our communications center was always tuned into, and on September 11, 2001, it became our window into the unfolding tragedy. I watched live as the second plane hit the World Trade Center and later saw the towers collapse. The immediacy of CNN’s coverage made the events feel very real and surreal at the same time. It was the source of all the breaking news that day for us.

explicit_text: Connie Rudd was our assistant superintendent and played a key role during the September 11 attacks. She received the call from the acting regional director instructing us to shut down parks in the northeast region. Connie quickly gathered management, law enforcement, and supervisors like Robbie Brockwell to make critical decisions about evacuations and park operations. I remember her sitting quietly, reflecting on the significance of the day, especially noting the date 9/11. She was central to coordinating our response and ensuring we stayed open as a sanctuary.

explicit_text: Robbie Brockwell was our concessions manager and liaison, and on September 11, 2001, he was part of the management team Connie Rudd pulled together to decide how to respond to the attacks. We discussed whether to evacuate lodges and campgrounds, given the uncertainty of the situation. Robbie’s role was important in coordinating with concessions and visitor services during this tense time. We worked closely to ensure the safety and well-being of visitors while keeping the park open.

explicit_text: On the morning of September 11, one of my best friends was supposed to fly into Dulles International Airport. She was actually sitting on a plane at Las Vegas Airport when the first plane hit the World Trade Center, and they were evacuated without explanation. I was the one who eventually told her what had happened, as she was trying to figure out if she could fly into another airport. It was a very unsettling experience knowing she was supposed to arrive at Dulles just hours after the attacks.

I was the one who eventually told her what had happened, as she was trying to figure out if she could fly into another airport. It was a very unsettling experience knowing she was supposed to arrive there just hours after the attacks.

explicit_text: My friend was at Las Vegas Airport on September 11, 2001, about to board a plane to Dulles when the attacks happened. They evacuated the plane without telling passengers what was going on, which left everyone confused. She left me a voicemail explaining the situation, and I was able to inform her about the attacks on the World Trade Center and the Pentagon. It was a strange and tense moment, especially since it was her first vacation since having a child.

explicit_text: My friend and her husband live near Capital Reef National Park in Utah, and she was flying out from Las Vegas Airport to visit them on September 11, 2001. It was supposed to be her first vacation since having a child two years earlier. The attacks disrupted her travel plans completely, and I ended up being the person who updated her about what was happening. It was a reminder of how far-reaching the impact of that day was, even to places like Capital Reef.

explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I learned that one of the planes had flown into the Pentagon after the attacks on the World Trade Center. This added to the confusion and fear about what might happen next. Connie Rudd and the management team considered the Pentagon attack when deciding to shut down parks in the northeast region for safety reasons. The Pentagon’s involvement made it clear this was a coordinated national emergency.

The involvement of that building made it clear this was a coordinated national emergency.

explicit_text: Shenandoah National Park was where I worked during the September 11 attacks, and it became a sanctuary for people wanting to get away from the chaos. Despite the regional directive to shut down parks, we decided to keep Shenandoah open and not evacuate lodges or campgrounds. Many visitors came in that first day and the weekend after, seeking refuge from the constant news coverage. I supervised operations at entrances and campgrounds, and I was proud that our staff chose to stay and support visitors during such a difficult time.

explicit_text: The September 11 attacks were a day none of us will ever forget. I was at work when I first heard a plane had hit the World Trade Center, and soon after, I watched live on CNN as the second plane hit and both towers fell. The attacks also involved the Pentagon, which heightened the sense of national emergency. Our team at Shenandoah National Park quickly mobilized to respond, deciding to keep the park open as a sanctuary while supporting Washington, D.C. and New York City. It was a surreal and emotional experience that affected everyone deeply.

explicit_text: Washington, D.C. was directly impacted on September 11, 2001, especially with the attack on the Pentagon. Our management team at Shenandoah National Park decided to support Washington, D.C. in any way we could during this national emergency. Many people fled the city and came to Shenandoah seeking refuge from the events and the overwhelming news coverage. The proximity of our park to D.C. made the threat feel very real, and we took our role seriously in providing a safe space for visitors.
